Voidwrought es uno de los herederos del estilo Hollowknighttesko de Metroidvania. Osea que te encontraras con una exploración bastante abierta, lo que tiene sus pros y contras, jefes durísimos con los que tendrás que aprender por medio de prueba y error para derrotarlos, y multitud de coleccionables y misterios alrededor de todo el mapa. Su estilo artístico es bastante único y es correcto, pero no es lo suficientemente memorable. Al igual que su música, y n cuanto al apartado narrativo nos encontramos con un juego con historia prácticamente Nula, eres un ser que aparece en unas ruinas y ahora deberás explorar las mismas para obtener poder. Es básica y solo sirve para ponerte en contexto. Voidwrought es un Metroidvania que cumple con lo que promete y si te has jugado Hollow knight y te has quedado con ganas de mas, deberías probarlo.

Overall: Voidwrought tries to be Hollow Knight. Luckily for you if you liked Hollow Knight this game is a competent contemporary. A very solid Metroidvania that scratches the itch and will only take about 8-12 hours to complete. There are plenty of 'bad' Metroidvanias so even if this game is only ever just 'pretty good' it stands above a great many others.  Gameplay: Standard Metroidvania hack'n'slashing with regular traversal upgrades and equippable collectables. 'Spirits' act as bonus sidegrades such as passive buffs and combat augmentations, while 'Relics' are rechargeable secondary attacks or temporary buffs that run the gambit in variety and usefulness. Boss fights are refreshingly complex in some cases requiring a good grasp of movement techniques to avoid damage and don't simply let you stand in one place and wail on them. 'Ichor' serves as the primary currency that you obtain by defeating enemies and finding caches, and there are numerous opportunities to spend it on spirits, relics, and hub expansion as you'll be collecting a lot of it and won't punish you by dropping it upon death like similar games would.  Exploration: Quite expansive, encourages backtracking, and remarkably non-linear. Entire portions of the map are completely optional yet still beg to be explored, appealing to that itch to uncover the whole map and rewarding you with a new experience that exists for its own sake. The map is user friendly while a bit minimalist with stark square tiles and solid color region demarcation, serving its purpose simply without venturing to have an aesthetic quirk and not offering any undue help locating the more well hidden secrets. Traversal tools are a little clunky to use, one in particular being gratingly slow to use in the most inconvenient places it's supposed to be used and another practically making most before it utterly obsolete upon getting it. Sidequests are pretty much just static seek and finds and none of them progress with you as you explore the game.  Art Style: This game wears its Hollow Knight influence on its sleeve but ups the edgieness factor several times. The hand drawn style fits the genre well but it's not unique enough to escape the comparison. Each area's chief design philosophy goes against your expectations, and while that makes the world seem disjointed and mixed up it ensures that they are all visually distinct. There's a vast array of enemy types that fit snugly into their particular zone's aesthetic and you'll rarely find the same enemies pasted into more than one zone.  Soundtrack: Eclectic, to say the least. Regularly goes from near-Eastern style ambiance to chunky metal guitars and blast beats and everywhere in between in a not particularly unified way filling, in the middle with your standard orchestral score. This kind of leads to some breakneck changes in tone, going from the soft and steady notes of 'The Commons' to the ear bleeding shedding and shrieking of 'The Eternal Engine' to the bouncy Arabic liveliness of 'The Grand Bazaar.' Stand outs would be the ambient music for 'The Surface' whose haunting and discordant choral opening leads into a vigorous rolling rhythm which suits the treacherous and inhospitable region well.  Cast: Really lacking in an entertaining and charming character cast. While they all stand out from each other in terms of design each of them are particularly one-note and do not act much differently than one another. The NPCs in the main hub really only serve as text dumps for the unnecessarily mystifying lore. As mentioned above the static nature of the side quests compounds this sense of an impersonal world, since none of the characters develop or change as you progress the story. It feels like you are the only active force in the world. The only minor exception would be Ose the Archeologist and while they are noticeably more amicable than the rest of the cast they are similarly sedentary, and in all places on the map they could reasonably be encountered you instead encounter a depersonalized holo-recording serving only as a clue to the immediate puzzle at hand. The game honestly would have benefited from being a solitary and isolating experience like Metroid.

Voidwrought is a very interesting game; gameplay wise, I think it holds up to some of the greats of the metroidvania genre, and has a fascinating world and morbidly lovely artwork to portray it. I think the main area that needs some work is the writing; it was difficult to know what exactly the protagonist was supposed to be or what/why they did what they did, and the finale especially lacked some context which could have used some elaboration. In a market now saturated with great metroidvanias, Voidwrought suffers the sin of being "just ok". I like the aesthetics and movement in the game feels great, but there are too many things dragging the experience down to something that I didn't end up particularly caring for. - There are a bunch of messed up hitboxes, mainly relating to attacks that send shockwaves / spikes / etc. along the ground where the hitbox lags behind the visual effects. I partly wonder if this is an FPS issue. The other issue is mainly related to enemies with lunging attacks where it feels like their hitbox lags behind their models, so trying to bounce off of them with the downwards jump attack is a crapshoot. - There are various combat arena rooms you get locked into where enemies spawn in waves. There is no warning of where enemies will appear and enemy hurtboxes activate as soon as they do, so if you happen to be where an enemy spawns when they appear, you take damage instantly. This got me killed two or three times. - The game's equivalent of the [spoiler]morph ball[/spoiler] is exceedingly slow and annoying to use which is at odds with the movement being so satisfying otherwise. There are also a huge number of paths you need this thing for so me finding it near the end of the game was really irritating. - The handful of elevators in the game are very slow and also don't automatically reset to your side of them when you approach, needing you to hit a switch and wait for them if they're at the other end. Come on man, Super Metroid already solved this 30 years ago. In addition interacting with warp rooms and various switches throughout the game are also very slow, so I guess the devs didn't even think about how annoying it is to have all momentum sucked away each time you need to interact with one. - There's a "collectable" enemy called the Envoy which is hidden throughout the game, which has an animation of playing a flute and a description talking about it playing music, which would seem to indicate that you're meant to know when one is around through an audio cue. Unfortunately, they don't actually make any noise at all. I don't know if this is a bug or an oversight. (Edit: There actually is an audio cue, but it's only plays within a small range of the Envoy, and it's barely discernible from the music synths or ambient SFX, so it's just badly designed and further at odds with the fast-paced movement in the game.) - The game involves mutually exclusive choices, one of which is sprung on you with no warning or indication where upon talking to a character I was suddenly given a prompt to either show them an orb or activate it, with no further context. Showing them the orb was apparently a mutually exclusive choice with activating it. That one choice aside, you'll need at least two playthroughs for the mutually exclusive content. - It's another game with a cryptic story told through vague comments from NPCs and vague notes and descriptions which I don't care for. In  addition, there's no ending cutscene of any kind. Beating the final boss just sends you straight to the credits and that's it. (Edit: There's a true final boss which adds a few lines of text after beating it, and that's it.) I beat the game in just over 8 hours with 100% map exploration and only a few missing items. If anything I'd say it was too easy.

Bought this on a whim due to Hollow Knight nostalgia. It starts off a bit slow and fighting felt a bit annoying at first. After you pick up some more abilities, things really open up. The map offers decent marking tools right off the bat so you can mark anything that's an obstacle and easily find it again later. You'll have several moments of joy when you finally find the abilities you needed a few times so you can open the new paths that are available to you.  Difficulty-wise it's hard to say, as always with metroidvania's. I'd personally rank it as medium. Most boss patterns are learnt easily after two or three tries at the most. And if you're bad at learning patterns, you can upgrade your attack strength enough to make several of them trivial. I know I had a few where I felt they were too hard, only to return a bit later and first-try them.  For the achievement hunters, I managed to 100% the game in 17,5 hours, but I did so with save manipulation. The save files are in JSON format and if you read the names of the values you can easily determine which you should reset to unlock paths that were closed due to earlier choices. If you don't want to do so, you'll need at least two playthroughs to get all achievements.  For my verdict, graphics are awesome if you like the style (though I'm no fan of the protagonist), music and sound are top notch, I did not care for the story and ended up skipping most of it, exploration and levels are above average. I felt I got my money's worth and enjoyed my time in the game and would rate it 4/5 stars.

Voidwrought es una experiencia fascinante que combina una ambientación oscura con una estética inspirada en el misticismo del Antiguo Egipto. Desde el primer momento, el juego envuelve al jugador en un mundo sombrío, lleno de templos desmoronados, vastas arenas desoladas y cielos teñidos de un ominoso púrpura. La atmósfera está impregnada de un aura mística que evoca tanto la grandeza como la decadencia de una civilización perdida en las sombras del tiempo.  https://youtu.be/EHOp9aU_b2g  El diseño artístico de Voidwrought es uno de sus mayores puntos fuertes. Los entornos están meticulosamente detallados, combinando jeroglíficos, estatuas monumentales y un uso inteligente de la luz y la oscuridad. Las referencias egipcias no se limitan al diseño visual. La banda sonora incorpora instrumentos, canto difónico y escalas que evocan al Nilo, pero con un toque etéreo que refuerza la sensación de estar en un mundo entre la vida y la muerte.  El lore de Voidwrought es profundo y está cuidadosamente elaborado. Los fragmentos de historia que se encuentran a través de inscripciones, pergaminos y diálogos con los pocos NPC son intrigantes. Hablan de un reino condenado por su propia obsesión con la inmortalidad, y el protagonista, un ser creado a partir del vacío, se convierte en una pieza clave para decidir el destino de este mundo. Aunque la narrativa principal es interesante, podría haber aprovechado mejor el potencial de este universo con historias secundarias más desarrolladas o personajes con mayor profundidad.  Uno de los mayores atractivos de Voidwrought es la facilidad con la que el jugador controla al personaje. Los movimientos son fluidos y responsivos, lo que convierte cada enfrentamiento en un baile coreografiado de precisión. Las habilidades especiales, que combinan ataques físicos con poderes sobrenaturales basados en el vacío, son tan satisfactorias de ejecutar como visualmente impactantes. Este enfoque en la accesibilidad permite que tanto jugadores novatos como experimentados disfruten del sistema de combate.  Sin embargo, esta accesibilidad tiene un coste. Aunque los primeros encuentros son emocionantes, el juego pronto se vuelve demasiado fácil. Los patrones de los enemigos son predecibles y las habilidades del personaje, especialmente cuando se mejoran, resultan abrumadoramente poderosas en comparación con los desafíos que ofrece el juego. Incluso los jefes, diseñados con estética impresionante y mecánicas interesantes, no logran mantener la tensión. Para los jugadores que buscan un desafío, la falta de una curva de dificultad más pronunciada puede hacer que la experiencia se sienta superficial después de las primeras horas.  Resumiendo  Voidwrought es una joya visual y narrativa que destaca por su ambientación oscura y su inspiración egipcia, ofreciendo una jugabilidad accesible y satisfactoria. Sin embargo, su falta de dificultad podría desmotivar a quienes buscan una experiencia más desafiante. A pesar de esto, el juego sigue siendo una recomendación sólida para quienes deseen sumergirse en un mundo cautivador, aunque sea más como un viaje artístico que como una prueba de habilidades.

I found this to be a pretty fun metroidvania. Great art design and decent map designs to explore.  Boss fights were fun, though I admit feeling as if they either had too much HP or too few patterns considering that they can easily cycle through all of their patterns 4+ times before they go down.  Minor gripes:  - The ending was a bit lackluster  - There is a noticeable cool down delay when you try to do multiple double jumps in a row or consecutive dashes in a row, but you can adjust your play for things like that.   - They don't mark all of the boss locations so sometime you just find yourself blindly exploring areas you haven't been to until you run accidentally run into a boss - Achievements you can't get in a single run due to choice decisions you make, requiring a second playthrough to get all achievements.  Overall I found this to be on the easier side, but it would still be a solid recommendation for a playthrough.

Voidwrought is a game im not quite fond of, I cant really recommend it for people whove played more than 1 Metroidvania that isnt HK, I like the music and the art (even if its unpolished at times) but my issues are quite large:  It didnt give me a reason to explore its lore, it had unclear paths cause a large part of the game are sequence broken with one ability, basic mechanics werent explained throughly except "look you can pogo like in HK" there were NO HINTS to items youre missing other than the map not being filled out in the room cause you were expected to mark them down yourself (which can be missed), combat overly relied on the useable combat tricks to deal real damage (items you find in the world that are reuseable but work off a cooldown) to deal damage for you where maybe like 6 of the 30 or so were useable, collectibles had no real rhyme or reason to be where they were, you can get way too much of one collectible and then can only trade it in for currency and another collectible there isnt even enough on the map to buy ALL the items that need them.  Bosses had rarely interesting stuff, looped their same 3 attacks in the same pattern with no mixup, had a little too much health at times and they had some similar moves going on in hindsight, most unique thing was the final boss of the normal route but I cant be asked to do a true ending of the game to see those bosses cause I wouldnt even know what im missing (hell I dont even know where to look for.)  Overall I'm pretty surprised I got this disappointed with the game, maybe I missed something that was crucial to the enjoyment but it goes down as my least favorite metroidvania so far.

I want to be able to recommend this game to others, I really do, but I can't. It feels bad to say that after only 3 hours of Gameplay. But two of those have been spent on the same single moment.  Firstly, the good, the art, while very hollow-knight clone-esque, is still unique, and the world (what I've seen, at least) is unique, with the surface world being an unexpected twist I did enjoy.  Unfortunately, that is the only good part so far. The combat initally is standard fair, let down by high enemy health pools and a the "relics" that add spells being expensive to get from the merchant, which leads my to my second complaint in the initial few areas. The blatently misleading item descriptions.  The first boss drops an item that, according to the discription, can be used for the games fast travel. What the game doesn't say initially is that the item DOESN"T. WORK. So you'll waste several minutes trying to figure out why you can't access it, Then, you have to backtrack all the way to your starting location again, be told why it doesn't work, and go find the thing that makes it. All of this has to be done with 1 single health refill/flask per life, as unlike hollow knight, you have no way to refill a charge, and each upgrade requires 5 collectibles, which you're unlikely..  This leads me to the killer of my interest in the game, as that frustration was made worse by the Second Boss of the game. An Overtanky, fast, 4 phase boss fight, that expects the player to have mastered the floaty movement, perfect Pogoing of moving objects, and using the slide move (that cannot be used to avoid other enemies as it doesn't have Iframes for contact damage) and, on top of all of that bull, also spawns in enemies that take 6-7 hits to kill, and that prevent you from dodging due to the above issues. 2 of the 3 hours of gametime have been spent fighting this boss, constantly dying for all of the above reasons.  I'm certain from some of the reviews, that there is much more to see in the game, but none of that matters when I'm trapped behind a ♥♥♥♥ boss, and don't have any incentive to go and explore, as it's a backtracking slog without the fast travel.  If you're willing to slam you're head against the brick wall, go right ahead, but I can't recommend it as it stands.

If this was a bad game I'd call it a Hollow Knight rip-off, but since it's good that means it's a loving homage to Hollow Knight. Really great art, movement is satisfying, there's some upgrades I haven't seen before in this genre...  It's very non-linear -- I was going to say that it's a game with a lot of potential for sequence breaks, except the sequence breaks are so prevalent I think it's just non-linear. It seemed like there was at least two obvious paths to get everywhere, with a more available if you're good enough. I wouldn't be surprised if the speed running/challenge running community keeps this game alive for a long time.  The only big criticism I'd level is that you get way too OP by the end if you collect everything. Honestly I'd suggest, to improve your enjoyment, to stop collecting health upgrades after you hit 6 or so, and don't equip the vertical laser spell, because it makes boss fights waaaay to easy.  Speaking of, the boss fight design is kinda meh. I liked the Prince, but most lacked variety and the inability to respond to cheese (like the afore-mentioned space-laser).

Edit: one of the very few instances i completely changed my mind after was initialy a very negative impression. i stayed because of the incredible audio-visual presentation; aka one of the best OSTs on my drive, mixed with fantastic ambient and effect design and a somewhat unexpected art-style that still manages to transport the cosmic horror (Giger meets Lovecraft) theme perfect.  the main issue in hindsight is that the game kinda pushes you into a direction (at the start) where you end up in a place you realy should come back to later. meanwhile i recommended the game to two friends, who ran into exactly the same area(s) i stumpled upon (early); with the only difference beeing me already knowing where they should go instead.  this might/will lead to enemies feeling very spongy in general; bosses and trash alike.   as a general hint: Vaults. they are the most important thing imho. . each vault upgrading your general damage output substantialy and i do mean substantialy. i don't know the exact numbers, but it downright feels like a 30 to 50% net damage increase per vault you solve/unlock.   this out of the way: i absolutly love the game (now). in a crowded genre (2D metroidvanias) it sits on the top.  the world design is stellar and everything is filled with lore and athmosphere. every item has it's very own lore fluff text, npcs are out of this world and alien while still feeling conceptualy familiar and often enough hitting the cosmic horror note of indifference to "normal" live forms. the toolkit has mostly familiar stuff, but all with it's own void/cosmic-horror theme.  instead of turning into a Samus-bomb-ball you turn into a black-mass-of-void-energy, instead of a slide across the floor you turn into a mass of tentactles and black void energy, etc. .  the same goes for your spell- and perk-kits. there is a metric ton of different spells to find. from throwing bombs, to casting energy beams, summoning circles of light-balls or casting a gigantic sword. perks aren't just perks, they are "souls" and got most bases covered. you can even sorta kinda a solely spellcasting build using perks.  the alien design of the protagonist and the world, the spells and collectables all together create a wonderfull picture that despite it's comic book grafic, feels still thick with cosmic-horror athmosphere (and some of the stuff is still brutal in all the right ways).  as a metroidvania it also works once you get the early game going. there is hidden stuff everyhwhere and the world and it's mechanics cryptic in all the best ways. when it comes to exploration i think this might by now be my favorite in the genre.  think Bloodborne, meets Alien, meets Lovecraft/Giger. you will talk to absolute outofthisworld-things, with deranged outer-plane-beeings and scientists that went to far. in a way even having different factions that you can "help", that all seem to have their very own motive (like the Grandbazaar and the Grey Pyramid). meanwhile not just improving your character but also re-building The Shrine, which opens up new options, like shops, upgrades or collectables and much more.   there are still some issues, like some bosses beeing to much; to much stuff thrown around, to fast; and also some boss-ways are to long. which feels kinda like a waste of time. but the power-grind you go through it real and you will have a much easier time once you get some spells, vaults and traversal skills.

So, I did not like this game at first especially when I got to the Lost-Time scholar boss. I just beat the game and I love it. I was not use to the moves of the bosses in this game, different from other Metroidvania's. The bosses seem impossible at first but you learn their patterns and no problem to achieve their defeat. Pretty nice game that's heavily inspired by Hollow Knight and leans in on the Lovecraftian elements of it. It holds your hand up to the first boss and then just sets you loose. It doesn't do the thing HK did where you need to find and purchase pieces of the map, but the number of branching paths creates the same feeling of being lost that people either love or hate. There's lots of hidden stuff and backtracking, and that's boosted by many traversal challenges having multiple solutions. So, things you miss on the first pass might be very different than someone else's play through because you had different power-ups or found a clever way to use one  My complaint is that the later bosses have a lot of RNG spam, ambiguous hitboxes, etc. They're still mostly pattern-based, but the developers added annoyances to make them seem harder and are then going back and nerfing the health and damage of the bosses (and in a few cases toning down randomness) to balance completion rates. It ends up feeling like you're being graded on a curve. It's not a huge issue, but I could see it being one for people who want Soulsborne bragging rights or who still fall on the wrong side of the curve

[h3]Summary:[/h3]  [i]Voidwrought[/i] is a 2D action-platformer inspired by similar souls-like metroidvania games, and developed by Powersnake. Taking place in a hand-drawn world of cosmic horrors, you play as a shapeshifting protagonist on a mission to dethrone a lineup of false gods who apparently all missed the memo on staying in their divine lanes. But it’s not all smiting and slicing; you also get to expand your very own shrine, because who doesn’t want a cosy cult base for your loyal, slightly unhinged followers? With smooth controls and customisable playstyle, [i]Voidwrought[/i] satisfies the thirst for those looking to explore yet another fascinating labyrinth in the metroidvania subgenre.  [h3]Gameplay | The Experience:[/h3] Rating: ★★★★☆ The game is fast-paced, action-platforming at its finest, where you’ll dash, leap, and cling to walls like a caffeinated spider. With multiple multidimensional worlds being your playground, every nook and cranny could lead to a hidden treasure or secret room, making the exploration aspect of the game one of my personal favourites. Additionally, there’s dozens of collectible artefacts to choose from to support your playstyle, but once you reach the mid-game, the overall difficulty takes a nosedive. Nevertheless, between slaying false gods and digging up rubble to expand your shrine, [i]Voidwrought[/i] offers an entertaining experience—even if it gets a little too comfy later on.  [h3]Graphics | The Visuals:[/h3] Rating: ★★★★★ [i]Voidwrought[/i] stuns with its hand-drawn aesthetic, blending eerie beauty with cosmic horror in every frame. From the opulent, corrupted Court to the chilling depths of the Old Waters and the grim leftovers of the Abandoned Expedition, every area is a visual gut-punch. The game also serves up a collection of eerie enemies that could be straight out of your darkest nightmares—or your weirdest art class. Honestly, the world of [i]Voidwrought[/i] is like exploring a haunted art gallery where every exhibit might try to kill you—and you’ll love every second of it.  [h3]Sound | The Music:[/h3] Rating: ★★★★★ The beautiful and spine-chilling soundtrack in [i]Voidwrought[/i] also enhances the game’s overall atmosphere. From the sinister hums of exploration to the heart-pounding battle themes, it’ll make you feel like you’re living in a nightmare... but a really well-composed one. It’s like your own personal soundtrack to existential dread, with music that pulls you deeper into the void.  [h3]Story | The Feels:[/h3] Rating: ★★☆☆☆ With deep lore filled with forgotten gods and doomed expeditions, the world of [i]Voidwrought[/i] feels like a dark fantasy mystery novel you can’t put down—if only you had all the chapters. Its backstory is well written but cryptic, and the main story itself is a bit… elusive. With no clear narrative to follow, you’ll often find yourself wandering a lonely, desolate world, piecing things together like a detective without a case. Furthermore, the characters each carry a sense of mystery, with unique designs and backgrounds that spark curiosity. However, despite their potential, these characters are often underexplored with limited dialogue and involvement in the main story. Overall, while the lore is intriguing at best, the lack of a concrete storyline can leave the experience feeling empty and detached.  [h3]Replayability | The Value:[/h3] Rating: ★★★☆☆ [i]Voidwrought[/i] took me about 12 hours to reach the true ending, which, compared to other games in the genre, feels a bit like leaving a buffet just as you’re getting started. There’s no New Game Plus yet, but if you’re the kind who loves tinkering with different playstyles or making different choices in side quests by starting a brand new run, there’s some replay value to dig into. As a proud completionist, I squeezed an extra 8 hours out of the game by scouring every dark corner and crevice, which I honestly found to be an enjoyable aspect of the game. Now, considering its cost and playtime, I’d say it’s worth it even at full price—especially with plans for new content on the horizon.  [h3]Final Thoughts:[/h3] As a fast-paced action-platformer, [i]Voidwrought[/i] excels in diving headfirst into cosmic horror with its solid gameplay, stunning visuals, and a mesmerising soundtrack. The combat is slick, the customisation is satisfying, and the lore is well written—though the main story is about as clear as a foggy abyss. While the mid-game difficulty takes an unexpected coffee break and some fascinating characters don’t get their time to shine, there’s still plenty to love for metroidvania fans, especially if you enjoy searching for hidden treasure in every corner of a beautifully-crafted world. Short but packed with potential, [i]Voidwrought[/i] earns a thumbs up from me.  [h3]Overall Rating: ★★★★☆[/h3]

Voidwrought is a highly competent Search/Action game, with an appealing artstyle, and an interesting world.  I will be honest, Voidwrought does not do anything you haven't seen before, but it knows what it wants to do, and does it well.  It is a Search/Action game that leans heavily on the "swipe your sword down to bounce of enemy heads and spikes" mechanic that you have probably seen before, most likely in Hollow Knight.  Especially early on in the game, you are not very agile and have somewhat limited movement options in combat, and mastering the downswipe is essential to getting through the first few bosses.  I had fun playing through Voidwrought, mostly on Steam Deck, and I really loved the art, especially the look of the main character.  Once you are powered you have a lot more abilities that let you move through the environment at a faster pace, making it a breeze to go back and look for more secrets, and there are quite a lot of secrets.    Good game, looks good, enjoyed playing it, well worth the purchase.  7/10

Overall it's a pretty good metroidvania. Decent world, exploration, atmosphere, and core combat. It tries a few of it's own things so it feels distinct, but also isn't overly breaking the mold here.  I'd say it's fairly solid gameplay wise, a few issues with some moves, bosses ,or lack if input queueing that cause occasional issue, but for the majority of your experience it plays well. The story is, alright, maybe a little lacking at the ending though. And if you want all achievments, it's just a grind fest, doesn't seem to have a New Game Plus to help make 100% collection of souls or relics or stuff feel more pallatable as you just farm on the same run in the same areas with no sense of new progress or goal beyond make number go up to get all the random things you'll never use anyway.  Shame that no NG+ part, could have given it more replayability. Art and audio is all good, not amazing for the most part, but definintely above average and overall makes for a good satisfying bit of play. Though in the upper right area of the map... the music exploring that place is just awesome, loved that part especially.

Tentative recommendation. The artstyle, sound design, movement, and lore are gleefully and unashamedly inspired by Hollow Knight to a degree that is perversely shameless. Unfortunately they're also worse than Hollow Knight on every axis. I do not begrudge someone taking a shot at the king and missing, but when so little is done to differentiate themselves it makes the comparison stark, especially when the dash is all but a T-pose with some lines behind it and the grappling hook sends you in random directions. Boss designs are fairly striking with unique attack patterns and visual design, but unfortunately the vast majority of of them are HP sponges who tend towards Touhou projectile spam and the healing in this game is both slow and anemic. The map is fairly well laid out, with void shortcuts offering interesting ways to blip across the map though the actual fast travel system has an extremely slow animation for reasons I cannot fathom.   It would be remiss of me not to mention that I had a fairly non-standard experience with the game, as I used an attack that makes a projectile you can pogo off of to skip straight to a midgame area and got both airdash and the Carrion-inspired ooze power very early. I did not find double jump until I had 95% map completion. I believe you are intended to find double jump about a third of the way into the game. I did almost every area out of order and the game was surprisingly cool with that, though it certainly didn't help the HP totals of the bosses to fight them with a below-average number of damage upgrades. I gave up on the final boss because even with every health and damage upgrade it was still an uninteresting war of attrition. This is not Radiance. You don't get opportunities to heal from successfully hitting the boss. Dial it back a bit.   Overall you could do worse, though even among B-tier metroidvanias I'd recommend HAAK, Biomorph, Moonlight Pulse, and Zau before this, to say nothing of Nine Sols.

Solid entry into the genre. It's always nice to find a Metroidvania with some new ideas, and while it doesn't reinvent the basic formula at all, it has enough interesting things going on that I can unreservedly recommend it. It has the pleasant quality of a well made Metroidvania where you always have something new to discover and new areas to explore, with new powers metered out at basically a perfect pace. I don't love the fact that all enemies respawn whenever you simply change screens, as this needlessly punishes exploration, but they die easy enough that it isn't a deal breaker. Absolutely worth your time if you love the genre, and a good place to start if you're trying to get into it (presuming you've already played Hollow Knight, of course).

Got the true ending in 17ish hours. Overall a solid experience.   The art style is lovely even tho it's a bit inspired to Hollow Knight, exploration is never tedious and it's a joy to discover new paths and new items. The soundtrack is a blast, seriously kudos to the composer.  I have two major issues with the game. It's super unbalanced, as soon you get two or three upgrades that boosts your damage you are literally immortal.   I think i killed all the bosses in one try, last time i died it was during the fight with the Eternal engine. Most of the times i died on purpose just to see the moveset of the boss that i was fighting.   The second one is a bit personal but prevented me going for 100%.   Multiple achievements from sidequests are missables because different outcome comes with different achievements, so basically you have one quest to complete but you can get different achievements making a different choice. It's not a bad choice because it raises up the longevity of the game, but its a choice that i don't like, so for the moment i'll keep my 90% completation, but who knows, maybe in the future i'll go for a second run :D.  Good job anyway guys, balance is what the game needs the most atm, but i'm sure you gonna polish it!

A phenomenal little metroidvania. If you enjoyed games like Hollow Knight or Blasphemous, you'll definitely enjoy this one. It feels a little weird at the beginning, but, like most metroidvanias, really begins evening out and opening up once you grab your first few abilities. It quite clearly draws a lot of inspiration from Hollow Knight, you can tell just from looking at it, but you can draw a lot of inspiration from something and still end up having it be really good. It's fun. The map is big and well designed. It has a lot of secrets. Boss and enemy design, variety, and actual combat are definitely big winners in this game. It's not super hard- at least towards the end. At the beginning it's quite punishing, but once you find some health upgrades and some good Relics, it very quickly stops being hard. The final boss is... a disappointment? To be honest, unfortunately. Especially when compared to some of the other bosses. The true final boss if you fulfill a few objectives is definitely a step up, but still not amazing from a gameplay standpoint. From a visual standpoint though it's a huge improvement. The world and worldbuilding is also quite fascinating, and honestly, with all that in mind, the trip is very much worth the mild disappointment of the destination, in my humble opinion. Got... just about 100%- not quite but basically 100% aside from a few things here and there- in about 12 hours. I'd say it's worth the price of admission, though my $20 is not your $20, so if you feel like that's not worth 12 hours, then you can wait for a sale. This game is definitely worth a look, though.

Voidwrought feels like a game made by artists. The game looks great, but unpolished. The menus look like something somebody threw together in an afternoon, but the rest of the game is gorgeous. The lack of polish is everywhere. Game design, level design are all mediocre, and a lot of it seems like they're trying to play it safe and just pulling game design elements and puzzles from other games without thinking how they should fit together. Enemy designs are beyond boring; this game makes extremely heavy use of contact damage as the primary threat in the early-mid game sections. You don't come into interesting enemies until way late in the game. The writing and narrative follows the same direction too: it's too much style over substance, and worst still, the actual storyline (both endings) are about as stock standard as it comes: you either sacrifice yourself for the bad ending or take over the baddie in the good ending. There's nothing complex about it, which is criminal, considering how great the actual setting could have been.  Now I'm sure you'll think this game sucks based on the amount of negativity in this post, but honestly, this is an average metroidvania that has great visuals and a setting that tickles the brain in all the right places. It's definitely enjoyable, just not mind-blowing. It's a fun weekend kinda thing, but I highly doubt it will generate its own legacy the same way Blasphemous, Ori, Hollow Knight, Metroid or Castlevania can. This will forever be relegated to the bin of hidden gems, and solid B-tier games when you've finished all the greats.

９０％ほどでのクリアをしてからのレビューです。 少し癖のあるメトロイドヴァニアですが、平均以上であると思います。 ホロウナイトなどに影響を受けた様子を感じることができますが、独自のアレンジを加えようとしているところもあります。  操作性：良好。左右方向に慣性がある一方、上下方向は動きが不自然であり多少癖はあります。 探索要素：壁を壊したり、隙間に入り込んだりすることが多い。好みは分かれると思いますが自分には合いました。 成長・スキル要素：序盤のジリ貧状態が長く、終盤になってからたて続けにスキルが加わり最終形態となります。徐々にスキルや成長を重ねつつ、徐々に進行する段階を大切にしてほしかったと思いました。 マップ：広すぎず、狭すぎずちょうどよい。エリアごとに探索完成が分かればよかったと思います。 ストーリー：日本語のローカライズや自分の理解力の問題かもしれませんが、キャラのセリフがいちいち長く、難解で回りくどいので、読む気がおこらず、ストーリーは空気でした。最後の最後まで全く理解できませんでした。全体的には良い作品と思いますので、もう少しストーリーの見せ方も工夫してもらえればよかったと思います。

I previously left a very harsh and nonsensical review that is now deleted. There are aspects to Voidwrought I do like; the art style and the overall atmosphere are amazing. I never made it far enough to see how the gameplay progressed but I'm hoping someday I will. This is one of the first Metroidvanias I've tried to play, and I found it hard to get lost and easy to progress right up until I got to The Council. I was recommended this game on Reddit as being a good beginner Metroidvania but this is not the case. The Council is a ridiculously challenging first boss. Now, I expect to die at least a few times until I learn the pattern, but when there are multiple phases, inconsistent hitboxes, and platforms that don't let you land on them half the time, it gets pretty frustrating. It's not like I've never fought a difficult boss before, The Lingering Will from Kingdom Hearts II was a very satisfying boss to beat. Dev, I'm hoping you implement some accessibility features to lighten the difficulty of The Council, or just make the fight less difficult as a whole (but that might make people who love the difficulty mad).

To put it simply, this is if Hollow Knight's more Eldritch touches became much more of the focus. You are a being of Void and Stone, and you've been brought to life in time for the Red Star, an event that brings your city back to the surface from the bottom of a frozen wasteland.  Now, with that out of the way, it's a solid Metroidvania with some of that Hollow Knight DNA. It takes a while for you to get some good movement tech, and unlocking a dodge that can actually go through things takes a while, but this is the game to satisfy that Hollow Knight itch.

(you end up with a zillion hearts.) And the new abilities you get overlap so that you can use one of the other to get where you're going.   That said, I really enjoyed it.

An excellent Metroidvania in just about every way except difficulty tuning. The first boss is going to be rough on casuals (especially due to the weird hit/hurtbox). However, the world opens up afterwards and a little bit of exploration and the Assassin soul make you an unstoppable killing machine in a few hours, capable of face-tanking anything, including the final boss.  [h1]The Good[/h1] +Colorful and well-animated +Really good music (on Steam, Bandcamp, and Amazon) +Great sound design. I love some of the sound effects. +The "base building" was kinda neat +Smooth movement +Huge open world after first boss and a bit of shrine unlocking +Two forms of fast travel +Couple of minor puzzles +Boss, save, and fast travel rooms usually obvious from doorway design +Multiple relics and souls to customize your playstyle +There IS an item to add iframes to your dash +Journal to track details on enemies, areas, journals, completion %'s +Multiple endings, and you can see all in one playthrough +Devs are communicative +Demo available +Can fast-scroll credits +Took me about 16 hours to nearly 100%, using a guide only to find one envoy +There are some minor choices to make, leading to replayability  [h1]YMMV[/h1] ~Enemies are very spongy at the start ~Some enemies have unusual hit and hurtboxes ~No punishment for death. Only throws you back to last save room. ~Game gets progressively faceroll easy after first boss if you explore ~I found some of the earliest souls to be the most powerful ~Likewise, only a few of the relics seemed useful to me ~No boss health bars ~No difficulty options, low or high ~Hard to restore health between save rooms (only a problem early on) ~Map isn't incredibly detailed (similar to Super Metroid) ~Limited number of map mark types ~100%ing is rather difficult and grindy, even with the item that shows map ~Story is pretty vague  [h1]The Bad[/h1] -Unskippable splash screens -Loud noise after loading a save (I think it's from one of my relics?) -The cadaver enemies' acid puddles are nearly impossible to see -Envoys could have been a bit louder, per their journal entry -A few item descriptions are unclear or even misleading -How to unlock fast travel is poorly explained -How to properly finish game isn't well-explained -Analog sticks don't work in menus (at least on my controller) -Your icon on map can block marks you're placing -Endings are underwhelming, at best -Can't place map marks in unexplored areas. Would have been nice to have dotted lines for void passages.  Played on: Win11, i7-14700K, RTX4060 Ti 8GB, 32GB DDR5-6000 @ 1920x1080 Using PowerA Enhanced Wired Controller for Xbox Series X|S

操作キャラクターはかなりキビキビ動いてくれて動かしているだけで楽しい探索重視のメトロイドヴァニア。 他の人のレビューにも書かれているとおり、かなりマップが広くてこれでもかというほどアイテムが散らばっている。  その充実したアイテムに対してマップ機能が貧弱すぎるので、アイテムコンプにこだわるようなタイプの人だと 同じ場所を何度何度も探し回ったりする羽目になってストレスになるかもしれない。 ストーリーはあってないようなものというか、頭に入ってこなすぎて結局なんなのかよくわからなかったし なんなら気がついたら終わってた感があった。  新しいエリアを見つけたときにどんどん探索エリアが進んでいく楽しさと操作性の良さだけで 最後まで進めたのでその辺りを楽しめる人にはお勧め。 それだけで楽しめなかったらやめておいた方がいいかも。  改めて書くと ■良かった点 ・キャラクターがキビキビ動くので動かしているだけで楽しい ・マップが広くて新しいエリアを探し回っているだけで楽しい  ■悪かった点 ・マップ機能が貧弱すぎて後から取りこぼしを探すとき等にあまり役に立たない ・いわゆる正規ルートとして用意されていそうな場所も壁を壊す必要があったりする 　誘導するような導線もないので一度詰まると数時間彷徨うことがある ・雑魚敵がやたら固く、せっかく操作キャラがキビキビ動いてくれるのに単調になりがち 　というか固すぎて序盤の歯車っぽい敵とかは倒せないギミックだと勘違いした･･･ ・ボスの行動パターンが少なく、せっかく操作キャラがキビキビ動いてくれるのにただのサンドバッグになりがち ・色々オプションの攻撃手段があるが、通常攻撃で殴ってるだけで完結するので存在意義がわからない ・ストーリーが頭に入ってこない

[h3] クリアレビュー。プレイ時間は14時間 Voidwrought の誘惑♡[/h3]  ねぇ、君。ゲームを探してるんだって？ だったら、運命の出会いってヤツを教えてあげるよ。 その名も――Voidwrought。  まずさ、君の目を奪うのはこの世界観だ。不思議な景色と静かな音、そして絶妙に整った操作性。誰よりも君のことを考えてデザインされてる、ってわかるはずさ。触れるだけでね。  操作も、画面も、翻訳だってさ、全部スムーズ。まるで君のために仕立てられた特注スーツみたいなもんだ。無駄がなくて、隙もない。…うん、まるで俺みたいにね。  けどね、この子は甘やかしてばかりじゃない。壊せる壁に隠し道、暗闇の中の罠。そういうちょっと意地悪なとこもある。だけどさ、君ならきっと乗り越えられる。 だって、俺がそばにいて教えてあげるから。  それにさ、デモ版も用意されてる。ちょっと試してみない？ きっと君、気づくよ。 「こんなゲーム、今までなかった♡」って…。

7/10  Voidwrought has solid fundamentals. Its two major influences are Hollow Knight (gameplay, art style) and Grime (environment aesthetics, music, mood). Voidwrought does well on all those fronts, so if you enjoyed those aspects of Hollowknight and Grime you'll probably have a good time with Voidwrought. That said, Voidwrought falls a little short of those predecessors without adding anything novel to the genre.  One thing that frustrated me about Voidwrought, and this is my personal preference that you may or may not relate to, is being confronted with possibly important decisions with little/no context and no help online. "I guess I'll just flip a coin and maybe be stuck with a disappointing ending" is not what I want out of my recreation time after a long day of work. I walked away after 10+ hrs without finishing the game for that reason.

Wonderful little Metroidvania very reminiscent of Hollow Knight that's occupied me for the better part of two weeks. Set in a Post-apocalyptic Aztec-Egyptian cityscape whose inhabitants have been forced underground following an unusual solar advent, with a nice mix of old fantasy and technological aberrations in its undertones. Gameplay is very simple in that you have a basic attack and two special abilities you can swap out as you find new ones, but I found it engaging enough, when backed up with the exploration and a great OST (please go listen to it if nothing else). At about the halfway point you become sufficiently upgraded as to render environmental hazards the biggest threat, so it may be a bit to easy for those experienced with the genre. My build started engagements by deploying a censor that rendered all enemies in an AOE more vulnerable to damage, then lighting my surroundings on fire with a jar of pitch. Cleaned house against most skrunklies and was very satisfying.

Too similar with HK and quite soulless for my taste. Fast movement but clunky attacking/dashing/specials.  You constantly see paths and items you can't reach yet, so you're continuously marking the map for secrets and dead ends with similar markers. But when you do find collectibles, they aren't even rewarding - why is there like 5 types of currencies for the same shop items? And after exploring and finding a dead end, I ended up just killing myself to skip the boring backtracking (no penalty or stakes here).  The "cryptic" lore feels like a cheap copy of Grimes plastered over. And the special attacks were mostly meh, not satisfying to use.  The game offers nothing new for me at least, it felt a bit tedious and perhaps even AI generated. Sry for harsh review.

実績を92%解除した状態でレビュー 残りの8%は全ての換金アイテムを集めたり2週目をプレイすることが必須のため諦めた  [h1]〇総評[/h1] [h1]素材は悪くないのに味付けが大雑把すぎるので結果として凡作となっている作品[/h1] 美味しくないわけではないので、今やりたいメトロイドヴァニアが無いなら買ってもいいかなという感じ   [h1]〇ゲーム内容について[/h1] オーソドックスなメトロイドヴァニア。テキスト量も多いが、何かわけありっぽい文章だらけで各人が考察してね！といういわゆるフロム系のテキスト。 正直私は好きではない。ストーリーなんてもっとわかりやすくていい。  [h2]〇探索要素について[/h2] 最初は行ける場所が限定されていて、新しいスキルを入するたびに探索出来る場所が増えていくという典型的なメトロイドヴァニア。 ただし隠し通路がかなり多い。しかもどれも分かり辛い。 分かり辛い隠し通路を何とか探し出して手に入るのが重要アイテム（体力増強アイテムとか、ストーリー関係文書とか）ならまだわかるが、 ただの換金アイテムであることも多い。分かりにくい隠し通路をやっと発見した結果がただの換金アイテムだととても萎える。 そしてすべての隠し通路を発見する実績もあるのに、隠し通路の場所がわかるようになるアイテムや装備もない。 全部自力でも見つけろということだ。私は全部発見するのは諦めた。  一応マップを見ればエリアごとにあとどれだけ取り残しがあるのかわかるのだが、その分類が大雑把すぎる。 体力増強アイテム、ストーリー関係文書、換金アイテムの全て合わせてあと何個残っているのかしか分からない。 「体力増強アイテム、ストーリー関係文書、換金アイテムのどれがが合わせて残り3個だよ！」と言われて必死で隠し通路を探した結果 3個とも換金アイテムだった時のあの徒労感と言ったら…  ワープポイントの数は少なめ。でも慣れてくれば敵を無視してサクサク移動できるようになるので数の少なさはそれほど気にならないと思う。  [h2]〇戦闘要素について[/h2] 全体的に大雑把だと感じた。  まず、敵の耐久力の設定がかなり大雑把だった。 他の人も指摘されているが、初期状態で最初のエリアの雑魚敵も5発切らないと倒せないほど硬い。 でも地上の敵が硬いのはそれほど気にならなかった。何故なら連続攻撃が可能なので連続で5回切ることですぐ倒せるからだ。 問題は浮遊する敵も同じだけ硬いということ。浮遊する敵は1回切る毎にノックバックして距離が離れてしまうので、  ジャンプ切り→ノックバックした敵を追いかける→ジャンプ切り→追いかける…  という手順が必要で、地上の敵に比べて大変手間がかかるため非常に面倒くさかった。 空を飛ぶ敵の耐久力は地上の敵に比べて低くすればいいのに…  次に主人公の強化具合も大雑把だった。 全部で4回強化することが出来るのだが、最大強化すると主人公が強くなりすぎる。 ダメージを受けると一定時間無敵になるので、その隙にゴリ押しすればどんなボスでも倒せてしまう。 ボスは色々ギミックを用意してるっぽく見えるのに、全部無視してゴリ押し可能である。 ぶっちゃけ強化が足りない第一・第二ボスの2体が一番強かった。  [h2]〇バグについて[/h2] 私がプレイした限りで遭遇しなかった。  [h2]〇実績解除に関する注意点[/h2] 相反する実績要素があるため（ある人に「協力する」実績と「協力しない」実績がある）1週目でコンプリートは不可能。 さらに強くてニューゲームも無いため実績コンプにはかなり覚悟が必要。 ただし強くてニューゲームは開発する予定があるらしいのでそれを待つのも手。  また「探索について」で書いた通り隠し通路が多いくせにそれを発見する装備もないので、全アイテム取得系実績が非常に大変。 私は諦めた。   [h3]〇まとめ[/h3] 素材は悪くない。グラフィックやアクションは良いものが揃ってる。 でも「探索要素なんて隠し通路がいっぱいあれば楽しいだろ？」とか「敵が硬くて強い方が戦闘も楽しいだろ？」 みたいな大雑把すぎるバランスのせいで総じて凡作になってしまったと感じる。 暇つぶしのゲームを探しているのなら買ってもいいんじゃないかな。

Quick Pros: -Fast paced -Dark but colorful aesthetic -Animation is fluid -Regions are distinct -A very good soundtrack  Quick Cons: -Some abilities feel imbalanced for progression making some others seem underwhelming -Game feels like its designed for players to achieve 100% but makes it tedious to do so -Could make item/hidden area tracking less frustrating by updating map or adding a Soul/item to signal something is hidden nearby  Game Mechanics Explained and Full Review Below: https://youtu.be/HxF-oDYmCcY

Solid metroidvania that is very close to being great. This game has a lot going for it - good level design, sound design, art style, collectibles, combat and movement abilities.... however i think it needs to be a bit more balanced. There is one movement ability that is just way too OP and renders a lot of the other abilities useless. And some of the combat abilities are much stronger than others. Bosses are probably the weakest aspect of this game. The first boss is awesome with multiple phases and different abilities in each phase. However, after that the bosses are just so weak and lame. The final boss in particular is just awful. I walked in and face rolled the boss in under a minute and the whole game just felt anti-climactic. I feel like most of the negative aspects of this game can be fixed with a little number tweaking, i really hope the devs can fix this!

Un très chouette métroidvania qui est très bon en attendant Silk song!  Une direction artistique magnifique, les visuels sont top et la musique très cool, un chouette univers qui me fait penser a un mélange entre Hollow knight et les souls like comme Elden ring et bloodborne a certains moments.   Niveau gameplay j'ai aimé, quelque peu rigide et glissant au début mais une fois les premiers mouvement débloqué tout est fluide.  Coté difficulté il y en a pour tous, si comme moi vous aimer le challenge sans recommencer dix fois, il y a moyen de prendre les salle qui augmente vos dégats mais n'en prenez pas plus de deux. Si vous voulez le jeux facile prenez les toutes, si vous voulez de la difficulté, n'en prenez aucune et vous allez faire aucun dégats!   13h de jeu de mon côté en écrivant ceci, j'ai terminé le jeu a 66% mais je reste sur ma faim! Je vais probablement y retourner pour le finir a 100%  Enfin bref, si vous aimez l'univers et les métroidvania foncez!

9小时88%进度通关。 优点： 1.弥补了在丝之歌没出之前的空缺的10小时 2.加深了我对空洞骑士等其余游戏类银河恶魔城游戏的认可度 缺点： 1.狗屎战斗设计：就单拿游戏中的第一个boss，四个阶段，尼玛四个阶段，我要a多少下你知道吗，模型判定还蠢的要死，站在台上还要脚滑，不卡边缘起跳再A，还有碰撞伤害，第一个boss打的就想退款，想着有点挑战就继续往后面玩下去了，结果你告诉我全游戏就第一个boss这么多阶段，捏麻麻的上来就喂屎吃。 2.冗余道具获取：全局游戏那些战斗道具我说实话就四五个能用得上，你可以说丰富多样化，但是你考虑到获取难度和能力匹配度的问题了吗，前期啥几把道具没有，有用的道具你做成不易获取的格斗室结束获取，然后司马存档点还老远，残血进去门一关被整死就不说啥了，从存档点复活连个死亡点标记都没有，我还得靠印象找坟，捏妈这些我都能忍，那你告诉我道具这种不放在开图的时候拿，搁后面通关了拿到所有的能力和增益后，我几把还要那逼玩意干啥？？？还有啊，藏宝室就四个，你给那麽多逼钥匙干嘛，我拿来开银行保险库吗？？？ 3.地图信息缺失：你要不就不做地图，要不就好好做，奥日空洞不都是详细地图设计，你搞个方块地图，有的一个方块藏俩东西，我还得回头再找一遍，标记存在信息缺失；npc不能有个特殊标志吗，虽然你就俩npc不在神庙，但是你该有的得有啊；还有啊，虚空能不能入口加标记，哪怕搁商店或者研究路那个研究员买标记呢，玩你这游戏第一次遇到我需要传送口做标记的，我真是谢谢你，让我养成了随手标图的好习惯。 4.能力曲线成长设计失差：boss战的设计是一定要和才获得的能力挂钩才有获得新能力的成就感啊，捏妈的你都放在boss战后面，那我要新能力干嘛，就为了开图啊，不能是因为我把获取点放这里了，你开到图了就拿，开不到就错过，给点提示和引导能力获取或者做好能力的广泛性结合这才是最重要的，比如我开到冲刺了，那后续的boss一定是要频繁冲刺才能艰难取胜的，说到冲刺我就想骂，内置CD那么长干什么，密码的我都有冲刺了结果内置cd这么长，你抄也抄完啊，获取完冲刺就该给冲刺大师了 总结： 给个6.5分，时无英雄使竖子成名，作为一款刚刚合格的类银河恶魔城游戏的话，凑活凑活也能玩，但确确实实存在一些诟病和不好的地方，后续出免费DLC补齐你多给的那些钥匙，给你7分，不然我真得严重怀疑你得到丝之歌发布具体时间了赶鸭子上架，害怕丝之歌来了没人玩你的残次品了，听见没听见没，我抄密码

Great Metroidvania game at a great price.  When I first played the demo several weeks ago, I didn’t like it.  I thought the controls were too floaty and didn’t like the fact that you couldn’t crouch/duck.  I went ahead and bought the game after watching an in-depth review.  I’m glad I bought it.  It has turned out to be a fantastic Metroidvania for its price point if $20.  It’s not the same caliber as more expensive games like Hollow Knight or Bloodstained, but that is to be expected for a game at this price point.  Lower priced games tend to have smaller much smaller production budgets, and that’s something that you have to take into account when comparing a $20 game with a $40 game.  Things I would have liked to have seen are a few hints scattered around on where you need to go next.  There was too much wandering around aimlessly trying to figure out what to do next.  I also felt very overpowered after getting a couple relics about 1/4 of the way through the game.  The combo of the two relics made just about every boss fight a breeze after acquiring those items.  Those items (aim sure the developers know which ones I’m referring to) should not have been available until well past the mid point.  Exploration was a blast.  It was always fun finding my way into a whole new area after thinking I had the map pretty much opened up.  If you’re on the fence, do yourself a favor and give this game a try.  It is one of those hidden gems that seems to have mostly flown under everyone’s radar.

Плюсы: 1) Приятный, хоть и вторичный визуальный стиль 2) Вписывающийся в атмосферу саундтрек Минусы: 1) Баланс сложности: казалось бы после прохождения множества игр фромов и основных столпов их последователей-метроидваний нервы должны быть крепкими, а руки расти поближе к плечам, но игра все равно умудряется раздражать своими неадекватно выносливыми противниками и странным распределением кривой сложности. В пределах первого получаса можно перейти от пассивных и неспособных ни на что врагов к арене, запирающей игрока с жирнейшими и надоедливейшими противниками раннего этапа. Про 5 фаз обучающего босса говорить излишне. 2) Отвратительнейшая система перемещения - местный перекат открывается в "обучающей" зоне, но ощущается бесполезным, так как а) не позволяет эффективно перемещаться в бою б) недоступен в воздухе, когда в первый же час вводит как летающих за пределами досягаемости противников, так и первого босса, находящегося в воздухе подавляющую часть времени. 3) Странный искусственный интеллект - я впервые сталкиваюсь с геймдизайнерским решением, что спавн противника по скрипту как носит контактный урон, так и позволяет им активировать атакующую анимацию без задержки. 4) Контактный урон.   Итого: Voidwrought не подходит ни вкатывающимся в жанр, потому что она скорее отпугнет их, ни искушенным, так как имеет столько базовых ошибок, не предлагая ничего взамен. P.S. Забавно видеть, как во время спонсорского стрима, транслируемого стимом, человек закатывает глаза и испытывает похожую фрустрацию.

Прошел игру, чуть не дочистил все локации, соответственно не все бонусы собрал. Но нашел всех основных боссов и прошел основной сюжет. Минусы: Каркас игры копирка Полого рыцаря - находим три ключа проходим к последнего боссу, а если узнали чуть больше по сюжету и сделали чуть больше, то у последнего босса открывается настоящий последний босс. Такая явная копирка расстраивает. Странная сложность боссов (возможно это лично мой случай) - сложным оказался второй боссв локации потерянной экспедиции, такой мясной таракан. Очень много траев на него потратил, остальных прошел с первого раза. Не повествовательные локации - если убрать дизайн, то все превращается в платформы и мобов. Не критично, но люблю когда окружение рассказывает чуть больше. Лор игры - видно что хотели сделать глубокий лор, но как по мне получилась непонятная муть. Наличие большого числа расходников - не очень понял эту россыпь ресурсов. Всеравно все равномерно добывается и тратиться, по мне как можно было упростить одним ресурсом или сделать более разные цены на разные предметы. Чтобы оправдать наличие разных ресурсов. Плюсы: Визуал - очень понравилась работа художников и аниматоров (за исключением анимации прыжка главного героя), все выглядит очень качественно красиво, локации просто бомба, дизайн монстров и боссов очень крутой, выглядит все атмосферно и эпично. Особенно запомнились ледяная локация и поверхность где горит та самая Красная звезда. Хотелось бы чтобы эта красота работала на повествование чуть больше. Звук музыка - дополняют визуал и все превращается в крутую атмосферу. Треки на на некоторых боссах и на главном экране огонь (жаль на главном экране трек долго разгоняется, услышал его полностью благодаря тому что долго сидел в настройках) Кто отвечал за звук просто красавчики. Лор так же поставлю и в плюсы - так нихрена не понятно но очень интересно) правда всю игру было интересно наблюдать что произошло и кто виноват (жаль в итоге ничего не понятно, может я тупой или более требовательный к разжевыванию информации, но было интересно) По итогу: Игра мне очень понравилась. В целом минусы есть, но готов закрыть на них глаза. Видно что ребята старались. Жалко что где-то не дотянули, но все-равно остались положительные эмоции и взахлеб прошел игру. Если бы минусов, которые я указал, не было, то игра была бы просто супер! Спасибо разрабам за крутое приключение и удачи и следующих проектах!

This is not a bad game. It's just definitely not a good game. Everything in this game felt tedious and boring. I love this game's art style. I'm definitely bias towards purple hooded figures as well so that gets extra points.  The music and overall atmosphere is awesome. In fact, they deserve more than a quick mention. I was playing this game on my Steam Deck at work. Sometimes I'd have to leave the game paused for a bit while I worked and I loved every track that played from the OST.  The movement and vibe are definitely inspired by Hollow Knight and it's welcomed. Combat is simple, but certain enemy designs still make for great fights. This isn't in reference to the "regular" enemies you come across though. 90% of your combat is stabbing your weapon at an enemy that barely poses a threat to you. But the boss fights I've had so far were pretty challenging. And the "dance" was really fun with these bosses once you understood what you had to do.  I know metroidvania fans either love or hate linear gameplay. I'm more of a fan of when games "nudge" you which way to go towards the next objective. This game has moments of "uhh where the heck do I go next" but also has moments where it literally highlights your next objective. So it's a toss up so far.  I'm 7 hrs or so deep right now, so I'll probably update this review once I complete Voidwrought. As of now, 7.5/10. Overall it's fine and not something I regret getting into. It's not a top contender, and that's fine too.  [b]Edit after a few days:[/b]  This is one of those games where I criticize it, because I truly think it could be great. I definitely have some feedback. I can tell a game has potential when I find myself criticizing it to myself or even complaining, but still playing the game 6-8 hours a day. Voidwrought is that. I have 18 hrs played now, and while I love certain things, other designs or decisions are questionable.  Combat is simple, I don't mind it, but most enemy encounters are also simple. There's plenty of cool little spells/relics available but I haven't ever needed to switch off the first damage increasing Relic they give you and this one I bought from the main hub vendor which sends out a vertical red laser. Everything gets obliterated by that build and I haven't ever needed to rethink my setup for different situations.  I don't mind "cage matches" in metroidvanias but I think this game could have sacrificed a few of them and instead added more bosses. A lot of these encounters I just spam my red laser and pogo all the enemies to stay safe in the air. I want to have trouble with a boss fight, then have that lightbulb moment when you figure out the right Relic and soul combination that'll give you a sweet advantage against that specific boss. This hasn't happened yet in my 10+ hours.  The slither ability is bitter sweet. When I first got it I was excited because I immediately recalled areas I knew I could backtrack to with it. That's great design. But then you keep exploring and this same ability trivializes everything. You even pass by areas you could tell were designed with a double jump or grapple point in mind, but who cares I'll just jump on this wall here and slither on over. It's also not satisfying or fun, you're really just slowly slithering everywhere lol.  I LOVE that fast travel is introduced early and fast travel points are plentiful. I love that save rooms and fast travel rooms are easily spotted while exploring with their purple/gold glows. I think the map is designed well and aside from the secret walls, it does a decent job of telling you which rooms you haven't been to yet. I think the excavation mechanic at the main hub is pretty creative and really enjoyed "coming back home" with money to see which new areas I could unlock.  I'm going to end up completing and maybe even 100 percenting Voidwrought on my steam deck, and I'll recommend it as a 7/10 game. But I think it could have been a top contender with a few tweaks and couple more months in the oven.  [b]Edit after completing the game:[/b]  I haven't 100%'d the game but I rolled credits after beating the "last" boss. Quotations because I'm assuming there's a "true ending" finale. The last few bosses were way too easy to defeat. I didn't die to them and I think that's an issue. I'm not some god tier gamer, these bosses just had very simple mechanics and low hp, or my damage was very high. Either way, that satisfaction of defeating bosses and unlocking new abilities or finishing a game was just not there because of how simple these fights felt. I rolled credits without unlocking all the traversal abilities. I find that to be weird. I don't mind when there's post-game content, but I think the pacing in this game could be looked at. I was missing more than half the relics and crests (spells and perks) as the credits rolled. That makes me feel like I missed out on content before completing the game. But the incentive to unlock all these missing items is lacking because what do I need new spells for? The bosses are defeated.   I think it's fine that the map is massive, but more often than not, backtracking and exploring wasn't as fun as it should be because you're pretty set once you get the slither ability and another that lets you punch out these purple bricks that often block important pathways. So I felt like I was exploring pathways _just_ so that my map was complete and not because I was excited to see what new upgrade/unlock was waiting for me behind the next secret. And speaking of secrets, there needs to be an ability that reveals all secret walls to the player once they've completed the game or something. It is _not_ fun walking around an already explored map and having to hit every wall to see if it breaks down to reveal another path. There needs to be more hints for the player that wants to 100% your game. Their time needs to be respected. There's already interactive maps to help players like that unlock everything, but 3rd party resources should not be required in my opinion.  I enjoy this game, but I really want it to be great one day. I'd love to revisit it and experience any new tweaks/patches/updates/content. If any dev reads this, I hope I don't seem too harsh. I really think Voidwrought is good, but I want to see it excel. You have an awesome foundation here.

[table][th] Review by [url=https://store.steampowered.com/curator/34633003-Gaming-Masterpieces/lists/] Gaming Masterpieces [/url]  - The greatest games of all time on Steam.  [/th][/table]  Is this game a masterpiece?. It is a decent Metroidvania, and there can never be enough good Metroidvanias. But it is probably no masterpiece. The game was programmed by a small team - essentially just three people. A typical indie game, even if the experienced publisher has ensured that many of the usual rough edges have been sanded down. You can also tell that fans of the genre were at work on this game. The visual similarities to the genre primus Hollow Knight are not entirely coincidental. The three developers are not rookies, of course - all of them have previously learned their trade with other developers and have now written their first game together.  Deeply hidden in or under a mountain range is a mysterious city. Few attempt to enter, and even fewer have ever been seen again after their attempt. We play a - rudimentarily human-looking - creature that hatches from the sacred egg cell. A talking statue tells us to explore the city and awaken its siblings... the Keeper of the Shrines explains to us shortly afterwards that the city is inhabited by corrupt beings and that he will support us in setting things right (and silencing the Council of Elders). He also wants us to gather followers around us who will support us. It's all very mysterious, but some things are explained during the game, at least if you pay close attention and follow the conversations. There are always encounters with friendly creatures with whom you can talk about several points - or you can just keep walking without spending much time on unnecessary chatter (and thus never understanding the story). Information you find is automatically noted in the compendium. You can find out a lot about your enemies and the strange town by reading through all the entries.  At the beginning, we can jump and strike with our claw-like hands. No dash, no double jump, no roll. All this (and much more) has to be learned first. As is typical of the genre, you have to continuously improve your character in order to stand a chance against the (relatively soon respawning) monsters. To do this, you can find and equip relics and souls. Dying enemies often leave behind Ichor, the blood of the gods, or other resources. A map is automatically drawn, and unusual locations are marked. The level of difficulty is quite high, especially at the beginning, but surprisingly it even gets a little easier as the game progresses. This is mainly due to the higher damage you can deal. At the beginning, your attack hardly deals any damage, and you must hit your opponents several times before they die. Of course, this often means that enemies can easily hit you, causing you to lose your life and wake up at the last save point. In general, you will suffer damage even if you only touch your opponents. The first skill you learn is a roll, but rolling through the enemy without taking damage is not possible. Your roll will be stopped by the opponent, and you will take damage before the enemy has even struck. The roll is therefore only suitable for getting away from the enemy to safety. Or to roll through narrow passages. Jumping onto the platforms is tedious. Even with the non-moving platforms, I always miss the mark, not to mention the moving platforms. Mega Man was easy in comparison! The bosses are quite interesting and, with a little practice, can be beaten. Provided you have the right timing and location, you can hit the bosses' vulnerable spots at the right time and eliminate them - and collect nice rewards for doing so.  [u][b] Conclusion:[/b][/u] Hand-drawn Metroidvania that contains varied environments and challenging boss battles, with tight controls and full of hidden secrets to find. It is no new Hollow Knight, but a well-made representative of its genre, made primarily for experienced players who are not afraid of a little challenge. Lots of jumping in dangerous environments and a variety of different enemies are your daily bread as you explore a mysterious city.

・探索：重箱の隅をつつくレベル。殴って壊せる壁やローリングでしか通れない細い通路が山のようにある。さすがにアイテムを隠しすぎかも。 ・戦闘：無強化の序盤は厳しめ(雑魚敵に5発かかるほど)。きっちり探索すればかなり強くなりゴリ押しできるバランス。 ・アスレチック：下斬りで剣山を渡ったりダッシュで棘の間を通り抜けたりする場面が少しあるだけで、それほど難しくない。 ・ストーリー：言い回しが硬く難解。正直よくわからないけれど雰囲気は良い。  叩くと壊れる壁が大好きな私にとっては天国のようなゲームでした。HP増加アイテムや買い物用アイテムが大量に巧妙に隠してあるのでさすがにやりすぎ感はありますが、探索要素&収集要素でお腹いっぱいになれます。見えてるのに取れないアイテム、スキルがあれば通れそうな場所、NPCの位置には絶対マーカーを置いた方がいいです。 装備品がかなり高額なのでなかなか揃わないことやファストトラベルがやや不便な点、マップに置けるマーカーの種類の少なさ(4種類)は少し気になりました。かなり探索偏重のゲームなのでそのあたりがもう少しよくなるともっと遊びやすいのになあと思います。

《星穹遗迹》，一款设定偏暗黑风格的类银河恶魔城游戏，严格来说，更像是对空洞骑士的模仿，大家苦丝之歌不出久矣，这游戏勉强可以成为丝之歌的代餐。但总体玩下来，发现它虽然画风不错，怪物种类多，但缺点明显更多，算瑕不掩瑜的作品。比如跳跃的下劈动作，用键盘操作会很难弄，需要先跳，再按下方向键，最后按攻击键，才能释放出来。而下劈这动作在这游戏里极其关键，免伤基本靠它，有些收集物也可以靠它提前拿到。另外小怪的血是真的厚，要普通攻击好几下才死，而且主角的攻击距离感觉有点短，经常因为碰撞而掉血。如果没掌握下劈技巧，这游戏的boss战还是有点难度的，第一个boss就有4阶段。而传送功能竟然要打完第二个boss才解锁，前期的赶路会非常难受，而且解锁区域需要大量的游戏货币，这就需要大量刷怪，强行增加游戏时间。还有过图的跳跳乐，可以站立的平台特别宅，很容易掉下来影响心态。不仅如此，地图收集率竟然不是显示在地图上，而是在纪要里，有点反人类了。这几个点很容易在前期就劝退玩家，建议优化下。 [code] No Responsibility Review，只根据个人喜好进行评测： [url= https://store.steampowered.com/curator/36266776-NRR233] 无责任评测[/url][/code]

It's decent.  Combat and movement feels good and fluid.  Combat is too easy as others have pointed out, final boss I had down in maybe 30 seconds and didn't take any damage while just jumping and spamming the attack button, so hopefully devs will make it more difficult in the future.  The world is pretty open and you can get powerups in different orders so it has good exploration.  Good music and sound.  You get a lot of different relics, which are essentially this game's magic, I felt most of them are pretty useless however because they are simply weaker than your basic attack which was disappointing,  There were only two I found very powerful for combat, and one that made travel easier.  Another thing I hope the devs change is make shops cheaper, I completed the game with 100% of the map complete but I only bought maybe half the items in the shops because I simply didn't get the cash, and that's with the item that increases money gain equipped most of the game so if you want everything you're in for a grind.  So overall, if you like metroidvanias you'll probably find this decent.  Maybe wait for devs to update based on all the player feedback.

Tough choice for me. But since I kept playing and beat the main story it has to be at least kind of good, right?  The short version: The beginning will feel pretty unfair. If you are willing to go through some frustration in the beginning and have enough patience until you reach a certain point in the game there is a good chance you can enjoy it later on. If you concider yourself a treasue hunter and exploring is your jam then this is for you. The exploring aspect in Voidwrought is great. Secrets can be very hidden. If you are here for amazing combat keep searching for another game.   The long version: To be fair, I started playing right after release. And the beginning of the game was soooo frustrating. Especially the first 5 gameplay hours in my case. The pacing in general didn't feel great and the difficulty balance between early game and end game is way off.   The early game: There is just nothing to block or dodge any damage. The only thing you can do is to keep your distance since you don't have any abilities yet, start really low on health and don't do much damage. Often I found myself in tight spaces where it's basically impossible to avoid damage. If you're standing in the wrong place you take damage just because an enemy spawns inside you. Not once throughout the whole game I managed to avoid that. There is literally no time to react once you see where they spawn. I mean I just started the game, go at least a little easy on me. Plus I have to say the movement felt slightly off in the beginning. I can't exactly put my finger on it what it was but I got used to it later. Combat sometimes don't seem to make sense since there is contact damage and you have to guess where the hitbox starts and ends. For some enemies, especially the first boss, it's not really clear.  I really wanted to love this game. The graphics are stunning! So I tried again and again.  Pretty early I noticed that the world is just too open to explore. The map is not blocked off enough by something where you need a specific ability first to proceed. You are not guided to the areas where you'll find the upgrades you need to properly explore the world. 80% of the map I feel like I used cheesy methods to make my way through. Hoping to find at least one of the precious ability upgrades. I actually ended up twice in a place where I couldn't get out anymore because I simply didn't have the right ability found yet. I fell down while fighting mobs and couldn't get up anymore. Reloading the save file was my only way out.  Well, that lead to A LOT of backtracking and wandering around headless, me guessing where I'm actually supposed to be or where to go. I felt so lost for a long time. The markers didn't help either. They told me where the bosses are - great. But how to get the abilities to be able to reach them? Good luck finding them early.  The end game: Since I was searching a lot for the abilities and couldn't find them I focused on finding secrets instead. I got my health bar complete. I also found all places where your damage will increase before I was fighting a lot of the bosses. Means now I don't need to watch out to not take damage anymore. I simply can stand inside the chaos and spam attack. My damage and my health are high enough to do that. The boss fights: No challenge anymore. Just spamming the attack button. The end boss of the story I defeated in maybe 30 seconds by simply pogo jumping on it. ¯\_(ツ)_/¯  Btw, the last abilities I found were double jump and wall climbing. If you've seen the map already you will feel my pain.  So would I recommend playing it? Not really to be honest. I feel like it needs a very specific type of player that will be able to enjoy it. I was patient enough in the end and I like exploration. Since I focused on the exploration part for at least 70% of my playtime I was able to enjoy it. That's the only reason why I give it a thumbs up here. Overall if I look at the big picture and consider all the above I wouldn't recommend it to a friend. The pacing and the difficulty imbalance definitely left a bad taste in my mouth.

It needs icons for things you've gotten already so it's easier to gauge where you've been, like killed envoys, and power ups. Other than that, I very much enjoyed the game.
